data.title: Moat Stocks Gain On Defensive Sector Strength
data.description:
In January, moat stocks found support as leadership broadened beyond mega-cap tech, with energy, materials, and staples leading and small-caps outperforming.

U.S. equity markets opened 2026 with a constructive tone, posting gains across major benchmarks despite notable swings during the month. The S&P 500 rose 1.5% in January, supported by steady economic data and some easing in inflation-related concerns, even as investors continued to assess shifting policy expectations under the new administration. Market leadership broadened modestly beyond the largest mega-cap technology names. More defensive sectors such as energy, materials, and consumer staples led performance, while financials, technology, and health care lagged. Smaller-cap stocks outperformed large-caps during the month, highlighting renewed interest in cyclical and valuation-sensitive areas of the market.

Within this market environment, the Morningstar Wide Moat Focus Index (the “Moat Index”) gained 1.2% in January. While the Index finished the month just behind the S&P 500, it got off to a strong start and was up roughly 4% mid-month, leading the benchmark through most of January. Relative performance shifted late in the month as concerns around artificial intelligence disrupting software business models weighed on markets, with a more pronounced impact on the Moat Index. Sector allocation, particularly overweights in industrials and consumer staples, was the primary driver of relative performance versus the S&P 500, while stock selection detracted during the month.

Smaller-cap equities posted stronger gains during the month, notably outpacing large-cap stocks as market leadership broadened early in the year. The Morningstar US Small-Mid Cap Moat Focus Index (the “SMID Moat Index”) rose 1.7% in January but underperformed both small- and mid-cap benchmarks. Relative performance was weighed down primarily by stock selection, while sector allocation was largely neutral during the month. Despite the underperformance, performance across smaller-cap companies more broadly reflected improving sentiment toward economically sensitive businesses at the start of the year.

Smaller-Cap Lead the Way to Start 2026

Moat Index Highlights: Industrials and Semiconductors Drive Gains

In January, relative performance within the Moat Index was driven primarily by sector allocation, while stock selection detracted during the month. Overweights in industrials and consumer staples were the largest contributors to relative performance versus the S&P 500, helping offset headwinds from security selection. While technology holdings featured among the top individual contributors, overall selection effects were negative, consistent with broader market pressure on several software and technology-oriented names late in the month.

Huntington Ingalls Industries Inc. (HII) was the top contributor to Moat Index performance during the month, with shares rising roughly 24%. The stock benefited from renewed investor focus on U.S. defense spending and shipbuilding capacity, following commentary around higher long-term defense budgets and increased emphasis on naval modernization. Morningstar continues to view Huntington Ingalls’ position as the largest independent U.S. military shipbuilder as a key source of its wide economic moat, supported by long-dated contracts and high barriers to entry.

Semiconductor-related holdings were also key contributors during the month, led by Entegris Inc. (ENTG) and Applied Materials Inc. (AMAT). Shares of Entegris surged more than 40% in January, reflecting improving sentiment around semiconductor capital spending and advanced chip manufacturing. Applied Materials gained roughly 25% during the month, as investors responded to its exposure to leading-edge wafer fabrication and advanced packaging. Morningstar views Entegris’ proprietary materials and high switching costs, alongside Applied Materials’ broad equipment portfolio and deep customer integration, as central to the durable competitive positions of both companies within the semiconductor ecosystem.

Other top contributors within the Moat Index during the month included Constellation Brands Inc. (STZ), a global beverage alcohol producer, and IDEX Corp. (IEX), a diversified industrial manufacturer.

Companies detracting the most from Moat Index performance in January were concentrated within technology, particularly among software-oriented companies. Weakness in these names aligned with late-month market concerns around artificial intelligence disrupting traditional software business models. Detractors included Salesforce Inc. (CRM), a provider of enterprise cloud software; Workday Inc. (WDAY), a human capital management and financial software firm; Tyler Technologies Inc. (TYL), a software provider focused on the public sector; Adobe Inc. (ADBE), a digital media and software company; and Broadridge Financial Solutions Inc. (BR), a provider of investor communications and technology solutions.

Moat Index Top Contributors and Detractors - January 2026

Contributors

Company Ticker Sector Avg. Weight (%) Contribution (%)
Huntington Ingalls Industries Inc. HII Industrials 2.80 0.66
Entegris Inc. ENTG Technology 1.24 0.50

IDEX Corp. IEX Industrials 2.57 0.31

Detractors

Company Ticker Sector Avg. Weight (%) Contribution (%)

Workday Inc. WDAY Technology 2.32 -0.42

Source: Morningstar. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Index performance is not illustrative of fund performance. Not intended as a recommendation to buy or to sell any of the securities mentioned herein.

SMID Moat Index Highlights: Materials and Energy Lead

The SMID Moat Index finished January higher, supported by strong contributions from materials, energy, and industrial holdings. Sector allocation was broadly neutral during the month, and relative performance reflected a mix of positive contributors and areas of weakness. More broadly, performance trends pointed to improving sentiment toward smaller and more economically sensitive companies at the start of the year.

Albemarle Corp. (ALB) was the top contributor to SMID Moat Index performance during the month, with shares rising 20.6% amid improving sentiment across the lithium complex. The stock benefited from signs that lithium pricing may be stabilizing after a prolonged downturn, alongside growing confidence in supply discipline across the industry. Investors also favored Albemarle’s position near the low end of the cost curve, with its high-quality brine assets and integrated production footprint viewed as advantages in a still-challenged pricing environment. Morningstar assigns the company an economic moat, driven by its low-cost lithium and bromine production, and believes Albemarle is well positioned to benefit as the lithium market moves toward better balance over time.

SLB Ltd. (SLB) was another key contributor, with shares rising roughly 26% during the month as energy markets strengthened and visibility improved around global offshore and international oilfield activity. SLB provides oilfield services, technology, and digital solutions to energy producers worldwide. Morningstar views the company’s scale, technological leadership, and extensive customer relationships as central to its moat, particularly as energy companies prioritize efficiency and capital discipline.

Huntington Ingalls Industries Inc. also contributed positively within the SMID Moat Index, alongside CF Industries Holdings Inc. (CF), a producer of nitrogen fertilizers, and Nordson Corp. (NDSN), a manufacturer of precision dispensing and industrial technology equipment.

Companies detracting the most from SMID Moat Index performance during January included Humana Inc. (HUM), a health insurance provider; Workday Inc., an enterprise software company; Fidelity National Information Services Inc. (FIS), a financial technology firm; Acuity Inc. (AYI), a lighting and building management solutions provider; and Atlassian Corp. (TEAM), a collaboration software company.

Source: Morningstar. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Index performance is not illustrative of fund performance. Not intended as a recommendation to buy or to sell any of the securities mentioned herein.

Choose Your Moat Strategy

VanEck’s suite of moat investing strategies is powered by Morningstar’s equity research team, which seeks quality companies trading at attractive valuations. The below ETFs offer access to US moat companies:

VanEck Morningstar Wide ETF (MOAT): companies with a wide moat rating, which means Morningstar believes the company is likely to sustain its competitive advantage for at least the next 20 years.

VanEck Morningstar SMID Moat ETF (SMOT): small and mid-cap moat companies.

VanEck Morningstar Wide Moat Value ETF (MVAL): wide moat companies within Morningstar’s value style category.

Important Disclosures

data.title: WTAI: Valuation Makes Me Lean Positive
data.description:
What if WisdomTree Artificial Intelligence and Innovation Fund (WTAI) had finally returned to acceptable valuations? A question that today more than ever I ask myself.

In detail, my interest is directed in 2 directions:

  • the fact that finally the geographic and infra-sector diversification of WTAI is rewarded by the market
  • and that the expected valuations have returned to acceptable.

And if on one side my analytical mindset tells me, “Perfect, right?” … On the other hand, the discretionary one asks me, “But don’t you wonder why?”

In fact, WTAI seems to have (at least from my calculations, therefore personal estimates) appealing valuations, and it is impossible that I am the only one to have noticed it.

Before understanding why this “mass amnesia,” let me tell you …

What is WTAI

WTAI replicates the WisdomTree Artificial Intelligence & Innovation Index adopting an Equal weighting intra-category methodology, with an expense ratio of 0.45%, a median bid/ask spread of 0.13%, and coverage by a distribution of 1.75%.

The strategy

Inclusion in the index requires that the company generate at least 50% of revenues from activities attributable to artificial intelligence or innovation, have a market capitalization not lower than 100 million dollars, and have an ADV equal to or greater than 1 million dollars. Once included, the index applies concentration limits of 5% per single stock in order to contain the risk of dominance, as well as a rebalancing mechanism at 40% in the event that the total weight of the first 50 components exceeds 50%.

Holding distribution

The result is 59 stocks, where the top 10 are ~38% (therefore the holdings are well distributed compared to other tech based ETFs).

On a geographic level, it remains a global ETF but is strongly US-centric. Over 75% is allocated in the USA, then about 8% in South Korea, and 3.5% in Japan (these are the main holdings).

On a sector level instead, in fact, a tech-heavy ETF with AI extension, considering precisely that Information Technology weighs about 69%. To this is added another large sector participation, that of Communication Services (16.05%): together over 84, which gives WTAI a direct exposure to digital platforms and technological infrastructure.

Peer

If we look at the other ETFs, WTAI has not been competitive in the last mega bull run.

The reason is clear: the more distributed structure of WTAI disadvantaged it in a phase of hyperconcentration towards big tech. In the same way, the exposure at a global level put a handbrake on the epochal expansion of US multiples of the last 3 years. I would like to remind you that, being a growth segment, the expansion of multiples is the number one growth driver of the tech sector.

When, however, we scale the TF to the last semester, well, we realize the fact that in a moment of repricing of the US market, having greater distribution, is actually an advantage; in fact, it becomes a more competitive solution than peers.

I think that this change, therefore, deserves a deeper analysis.

Valuations

According to WisdomTree, the forward P/E is 18.89x, relatively tight, a signal of the fact that the market is pricing in very significant earnings growth. Let's, however, get more into the heart of the situation: having available the holding distribution of WTAI and the earnings growth rates shared by FactSet on the US equity market, it is possible to make more detailed estimates. The calculations are expressed in this table

Aggregatively, it seems that on WTAI there is approximately an expected earnings growth of 21%. A growth clearly driven by AI monetization: the result of a distinctive CAPEX (+63% in 2024) and widening hyperscaler margins. In fact, considering precisely a forward P/E = 18.89, at equal P/S (P/S = 4.79), it means that the net margin forward ≈ 25%. So it is coherent with the logic: wide CAPEX + wide margins = earnings with growth rates above the historical average.

This is indispensable for the stocks of the tech segment inside WTAI, because, being growth, they have a very low total yield (dividend yield at 0.49% + net buyback yield at 0.49%).

The return is almost totally growth-driven; it depends on the expansion of multiples (otherwise, tech would not have expected return). Therefore, the fact that the expected multiples are below 20x is, in my opinion, a positive thing for expected returns (as long as they are sustainable).

My opinion

I am often criticized for having a too simplistic view of the market. In my operations, I maintain an opportunistic approach based on data. Basically, I like to "buy convenience" and "sell excesses" almost "with my eyes closed."

And is WTAI convenient? Based on GS estimates on the sectors of the S&P 500, and simulating a portfolio with the same holding distribution as WTAI, a FWD P/E close to 23x should come out. The fact that WTAI’s is lower reflects, in my opinion, (in part) the equal weight nature and, above all, global nature (ex. US FWD P/Es are generally lower).

Therefore, making a small stretch, and applying the GS percentile distribution of the 11 sectors of the S&P 500 to the holding distribution of WTAI, the result obtained is that the expected valuations are close to the 70th percentile of the 30-year distribution.

They are my personal estimates, which, however, communicate to me an anomaly: the most hyped sector at a global level, shows (very approximate) valuations lower than the S&P 500 itself. And at the lows of the RSI since November 2025. If I approached the market ONLY in an agnostic way, I would say that this is a phase of accumulation, not distribution.

Risk

But we are not algorithms, and I would add, fortunately, since if mathematics were enough to invest, we analysts would all be very rich. We are not complaining, but… it is not exactly like that. Earnings growth estimates take for granted that CAPEX growth rates will be maintained, together with those of profit margin expansion of companies. Otherwise the expected earnings growth rate, the product of these two components, falls, and valuations with little would become unsustainable. And the key lies in the CAPEX growth rate of 2024 equal to 63%, which stimulates the most capitalized stocks of the AI segment: semiconductors. If, for example, the CAPEX growth rate were to return to “normal,” the expected revenues of semiconductor companies would fall, making today’s valuations expensive. And what today frightens the market is the idea that the models do not generate sufficient ROI. And it is an element that is starting to weigh on a sector in particular: that of software (I talked about it here).

For this reason the market today does not cover this valuation gap in WTAI: it does not yet truly believe that the tech segment can generate such a high expected growth rate.

Conclusion

However, I repeat: I remain very anchored to the data. Guidance tends to statistically generate positive surprises in most observations. Therefore, thinking negatively is statistically disadvantageous in the market. Combined with the fact that WTAI presents aggregate and relative valuations close to the average distribution, it leads me to lean positive. Naturally, closely following the dynamics related to the software sector could easily invalidate the thesis.

data.title: IREN: Execution De‑Risk And Secured Capacity Drives Growth
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In my last article, I explained why the next 12–18 months are crucial for IREN Limited’s (IREN) performance and why investors should value the company as an AI infrastructure provider.

I also highlighted the company indicated a potential revenue growth of at least 90% in the following years. The latest report confirmed that IREN is moving towards this target, demonstrating a growth in expected ARR - from $1.9B in Q1’26 to $3.4B in Q2’26.

This article examines whether the company is efficiently and effectively shifting towards becoming an AI infrastructure player and why its focus on Capacity, Capital and Execution de-risk is a fact, based on 95% funding coverage for the AI contract with Microsoft; however, financial risk remains. IREN’s valuation still is somewhere between a mining and an AI infrastructure company.

In my view, the company does not sell compute power, but AI secured capacity; therefore, I continue to rate the stock as BUY.

Q2’26 Financial Results Overview

The bitcoin mining business remains the company’s main revenue driver, with $167M in Q2’26 reflecting a 47.5% YoY increase, while AI services with about $17.3M grew by 550% YoY.

Gross margins for mining and AI services are 62% and 86%, respectively. The total gross margin is 64% as of Q2’26, compared to 72% in Q2’25, mainly due to the lower price of bitcoin.

IREN’s profitability remains volatile, primarily driven by increased SG&A expenses, higher depreciation charges, and debt conversion inducement expense -which is a one-off expense- resulting in a negative profit margin of (19%). However, the EBITDA margin increased from 41% in Q2’25 to 59% in Q2’26.

Regarding debt, IREN has a net debt position of $582M in Q2’26 versus ($66.6M) in Q1’26 and ($135.7M) in Q1’25, reflecting the company’s heavy investments in AI infrastructure workloads. Notably, CapEx of $539.7M for PPE and $179.4M for computer hardware are key components of IREN’s investing activities.

Overall, the company’s financial position is extremely sensitive, and this is a risk factor that investors should take into account before opening a position. Continuously negative FCF, along with volatility in both operating and net income, puts pressure on IREN’s growth trajectory. For this reason, the company needs increasing levels of financing.

Execution De-Risk and Promising ARR

In my last article, I highlighted the company’s high execution risk.

Starting with Capital -as it is the riskiest factor- IREN has achieved 95% funding coverage of the $9.7B required for the AI project with Microsoft (MSFT). The coverage comes from:

  • $1.9B from MSFT’s prepayments and,
  • $3.6B from underwriting commitments from bank loans with interest rates less than 6%,

reflecting an average interest rate of about 3%.

Moreover, the company uses various sources of funding in order to keep its balance sheet and liquidity at appropriate levels, given that its business model is heavily capital-intensive.

The company’s management stated:

Firstly, we secured underwriting commitments for $3.6 billion of GPU financing at an interest rate of less than 6%. Together with customer prepayments, this provides funding coverage for approximately 95% of the GPU-related CapEx supporting our $9.7 billion AI contract with Microsoft… As Dan has highlighted earlier, we have now secured a $3.6 billion delayed draw term loan financing package from Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan… and it will be secured against the underlying GPUs and the contracted cash flows from Microsoft, which supports a strong credit profile and an attractive interest rate expected to be less than 6%... So with the announcement today of the GPU financing, we've now secured 95% of the cost of the GPUs at an average interest rate of around 3% when you factor into the prepayment.

Turning to the second C, the capacity, IREN has already secured more than 4.5GW of fully secured capacity, which means it has already secured—but not yet built - the data center buildings, the grid interconnection, the AI-related hardware (i.e., GPUs, air-cooled systems), and overall all the parts a data center needs in order to be built and operate.

I mentioned the air-cooled system with purpose. IREN mentioned that they are seeing increased interest in air‑cooled capacity, because this tech can be implemented rapidly and with minimal additional capital needs. As a result, with the air-cooled system, IREN will be able to offer full scale solutions to its clients in order to meet the strong demand for AI infrastructure.

Notably, the company stated:

Another factor that we mentioned earlier, we are seeing an increased interest in air cooled capacity, And that is primarily because that can fill immediate needs, especially within our portfolio because we have existing operational data centers on an air-cooled basis that are capable of hosting GPUs in relatively short order with relatively minimal capital upgrades… At Prince George, the data center fit-outs for air-cooled, NVIDIA B200 and B300 GPUs are now complete and awaiting the delivery of the remaining GPUs on order… The increased focus on air-cooled deployments aligns extremely well with our existing footprint of 810 megawatts of already operational air-cooled data centers.

I want to mention here another risk factor. According to IREN the target of $3.4B ARR reflects utilization of only about 10% of secured capacity. This seems promising in terms of growth potential but comes with the risk that the company may not manage to fully contract this capacity. As we’ll discuss later, the company notes that it has pricing power and can choose its clients; however, that does not mean that any slowdown in the AI market - combined with its capital‑heavy business model - would not affect its performance.

In my view, the most important factor is the last C, Customers. IREN is in negotiations with other hyperscalers and large AI enterprises. However, given that IREN has secured more than 4.5GW of capacity -in a period of stressed capacity availability due to high demand—the company has both pricing power and a time‑to‑market advantage.

Power is the first limitation in the AI market, as mentioned by the company, with analysts estimating that data center power usage will significantly increase in the following years. For this reason, MSFT's CEO noted that "The biggest issue we are now having is not a compute glut, but it’s power."

Power limitation, vertically integrated solutions, and secured capacity will drive the company’s growth trajectory in the following years. Notably, the company stated:

We have multiple advanced negotiations underway for larger scale deployments and are also seeing hyperscalers and AI enterprises increasingly focus on air-cooled GPUs given the faster deployment time lines… With capital access now demonstrated at scale, we're able to engage customers with greater flexibility on how and when we bring new capacity to market while maintaining discipline around pricing and partner selection.

All the above contributes to IREN holding $2.3B ARR under contract and targeting $3.4B ARR in CY26. In my last article, I roughly estimated a potential revenue growth of at least 90%, in the following years, based on the company’s ARR of $1.9B -which means that IREN has almost doubled its ARR expectation. Therefore, my estimation of 90% potential revenue upside, remains more than possible in the coming years, based on the company’s guidance.

Bottom Line

From a valuation perspective, IREN seems overvalued compared to the sector. Both P/E and EV/Sales ratios indicate a strongly overvalued company.

In growth terms, IREN is projected to deliver impressive revenue growth in the following years, making its FWS P/S ratio more than attractive.

The stock appears overvalued and already priced with the MSFT deal news. However, in my view, the current stock price does not fully reflect the company’s potential, especially after the significant execution de-risk.

I believe that investors need to see the company not only as an AI infrastructure player but also as a fully secured capacity provider that offers plug-and-play solutions. Risk analysis includes financial risk and still some execution - though at lower levels - which could affect the company’s overall performance. But, in my view, the company can overcome these risks through its 3Cs strategy.

Overall, I maintain a BUY rating on the stock, but I suggest it only for investors with high-risk tolerance and only as a small portion of their portfolios.

data.title: Digital Realty Q4 2025: Stability And Growth Without Surprises
data.description:
Today, we will analyze one of the largest global data center operators - Digital Realty Trust (DLR). We will look at whether there have been any changes in the company's financial metrics or credibility in Q4 2025. If one wants to see our last article for this REIT, here is the link.

Digital Realty Trust In Details (Q4)

Digital Realty had total assets of approximately $49.4 billion as of the fourth quarter of 2025. The current market capitalization is close to $61 billion. The total debt of the company is around $19.65 billion, and the preferred equity is ~ $731.6 million. DLR has over 300 data centers and over 55 metros.

The company falls in the "investment-grade" category, as you can see from its credit ratings from the major credit agencies below:

And in the next picture, you see the debt profile of DLR:

Financial Metrics

Using the information from the earnings, we will calculate some important financial metrics for the company:

  • Asset Yield ((total revenue - operating expenses + depreciation and amortization) / total assets): 5.66%.
  • Asset Coverage Ratio (total assets/debt): 251%.
  • Operating Expenses/Total Revenue (excluding depreciation and amortization): 54%.
  • Interest Expenses/Debt: 2.23%.
  • Rental Revenue: 8.27%

We also calculate market-adjusted asset yield to be more accurate. Therefore, we will need a price-to-book value, which is, at the time of writing, 2.70. We replace the book value of equity with its market value by applying the current Price/Book. From here, we can calculate the market-adjusted yield and the market-adjusted asset coverage ratio.

  • Market Adjusted Asset Yield: 3.05%.
  • Market Adjusted Asset Coverage ratio: 466.28%.
  • Market Adjusted Rental Revenue (lease revenue/market-adjusted total assets) gross on leasing. : 4.46%
  • Expected AFFO Yield: 4.06%

Credibility

We already showed the credit ratings of DLR - "Baa2" by Moody's, "BBB+" by S&P, and "BBB" by Fitch. But now we want to check in detail its credit metrics, and we will use the Moody's approach:

- The gross assets of DLR are close to $59.4 billion, or, using Moody's scale, this is equivalent to an "Aa1" credit rating.

- The unencumbered assets are around 97.8%, or equivalent to an "Aa2" credit rating.

- The secured debt is close to 1.4% of the gross assets, or this is equivalent to an "Aa2" credit rating.

- The total debt + preferred stock is approximately 32% of the gross assets, or this is a "Baa1" credit rating.

- Net debt to EBITDA: We use the total debt here, and with an EBITDA of $2.76 billion, the ratio is close to 5.8, or a "Baa3" credit rating.

- Fixed charge coverage: EBITDA covers interest and preferred stock dividends at a rate of nearly 6 times, an "A2" credit rating equivalent.

- Market positioning, asset quality, and operating environment: A1.

- Liquidity and access to capital - The operating expenses represent about 54% of revenue, and EBITDA covers interest and preferred share dividends nearly 6 times, indicating strong earnings capacity. Here, the credit rating is equivalent to a "Baa3."

After calculating all ratios based on market-adjusted characteristics, we obtain an A1 credit rating. In our opinion, DLR deserves a higher credit rating than what Moody's has given it and is most likely one of the safest companies in the sector.

Financial Instruments of the Company

  • Preferred Stocks

(DLR.PR.J), (DLR.PR.K) and (DLR.PR.L) trade below par at the time of writing. The call dates of the stocks have passed, so they are callable. Moody's rates them with a "Baa3" credit rating, S&P with "BBB-", and Fitch with "BB+". All three preferred stocks have close to 6.18 -6.32% current yields. The next charts show their potential prices vs current yields. At the current moment, we prefer to "hold" them.

DLR-J:

DLR-L:

DLR-K:

  • OTC Bonds:

Below are shown the over-the-counter bonds of DLR:

Moody's gives a credit rating of "Baa2". The highest yield to maturity is close to 4.74%, with a spread to the treasury yield of around 1%.

  • Common stock

The common stock of DLR is traded at a price of $174.57, and we can see that in recent months it recorded some gains.

Conclusion

Digital Realty Trust continues to demonstrate financial stability, credibility, and growth in Q4 2025. Overall, no significant negative changes in metrics or credibility (no change in credit ratings either). For now, we recommend a "hold" for preferred stocks.

data.title: Illinois Tool Works: Cyclical Recovery And Structural Growth Improvements Should Drive Upside
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I previously covered Illinois Tool Works (ITW) last month, and the stock has delivered a good double-digit return since then versus flat performance by the S&P 500 (SPY). Moving forward, I remain optimistic about ITW's growth prospects. ITW's revenue outlook is positive, with the short-cycle industrial market continuing to show early signs of recovery and the company seeing strengthening orders and backlogs. In addition, the company's customer-backed innovation initiatives continue to gain traction, and its product line simplification drag is reducing, which should also structurally improve the company's growth profile.

On the margin front, the company should benefit from operating leverage and continued cost benefits from enterprise initiatives. While ITW trades at a modest premium vs historical levels, I believe this valuation is justified, given structural improvement in growth profile, good execution, and early phases of cyclical recovery. Overall, I find risk-reward attractive and continue to rate the stock a buy.

Recent Financial Performance

ITW reported USD 4.1 billion in revenues in Q4 2025. This was up 4.1% Y/Y on a reported basis and 1.3% year over year organically. Segmentwise, all segments except construction products reported positive revenue growth in the quarter. Automotive OEM segment revenue grew 6% year over year, with China showing good strength. In the Food and Equipment segment revenues were helped by good growth in the services business, while the equipment business was flat. In the Test and Measurement/Electronics segment, the electronics business was flat due to tough Y/Y comparisons, while the test and measurement business saw a three percent year-over-year growth. In the Welding segment, equipment and filler metals showed good strength, while consumables were flat. In the Polymer and Fluid segment, the company benefitted from new product launches in the automotive aftermarket. The Specialty Products segment revenue benefited from strong equipment growth, which was partially offset by consumables decline.

The only segment that was down last quarter was the Construction Products which was negatively impacted by a decline in North American residential renovation spending and a slowdown in Europe.

On the margin side, the company's operating margin expanded 30 bps Y/Y to 26.5 percent, with enterprise initiatives contributing 140 basis points. The segment operating margin was up 120 bps year-over-year to 27.7 percent, with all seven segments reporting improved operating margins driven by enterprise initiatives.

Outlook

Moving forward, I am optimistic about ITW's growth prospects, supported by early signs of recovery in its several short-cycle industrial end markets and continued execution on its innovation-led growth strategy.

In my last article, I talked about the interest rate cycle reversal catalyzing cyclical recovery for the company. This has already started taking place. The company reported sequential revenue growth of 4% from Q3 25 to Q4 25. This is substantially above the company’s historical sequential trend of 2%, indicating improved demand conditions.

On its last earnings call, management noted broad-based improvement in demand across end markets and called what they were seeing “more than green shoots.” They also noted improving orders and backlog trends across parts of the portfolio, including the semiconductors, welding, and general industrials. The semiconductor and electronics market appears to be in early stages of recovery, with China-related capex freeze that impacted results earlier in 2025 easing and the semiconductor capex cycle in early phases of recovery. Industrial demand is also improving, with general industrial orders up across both test and measurement and welding segments.

Most of ITW's portfolio is exposed to short cycle industrial markets where improving orders translate into revenue growth with a short lag. So this order recovery should translate into a good revenue growth for the company in 2026. In addition, I am expecting further rate cuts as 2026 progresses, which should further strengthen recovery in construction, industrial, and automotive end markets.

In addition to cyclical recovery, ITW should continue to benefit from customer-back innovation (CBI). CBI contributed 2.4% to organic growth in 2025, up 40 basis points year over year, and management expects further improvement in 2026 and beyond. One of the leading indicators of future CBI-driven revenue is patent filings. The company's patent filings have increased meaningfully in recent years and were up 9% Y/Y in 2025 following an 18% Y/Y increase in 2024. The company's innovation model is tied directly to solving customer problems, and this increased patent filing indicates a strengthening pipeline of customer-specific solutions. So I'm expecting the increased patent filing in recent years to translate into differentiated products and more new launches over the coming years. This should help in further accelerating CBI-backed sales.

One area that is a good example of the company's solid execution of CBI initiatives is EV markets. This market needs specialized fasteners, components, and assemblies used in battery packs, lightweight structures, and noise reduction applications. ITW has done a good job in terms of addressing these customer needs, and it now has more than $300 of content per EV compared to roughly $220 per traditional internal combustion engine vehicle. This content advantage becomes even more meaningful when combined with ITW's geographic exposure in the EV market. China accounts for roughly 65% of global EV production, and the company has done a good job in terms of strengthening its position in China, especially with local manufacturers. This position is ITW well to outgrow the EV market growth in the long term.

In addition to these growth areas, the company should also benefit from reduced headwinds from product line simplification (PLS) initiatives moving forward. After the last several years of aggressive portfolio pruning, PLS is expected to be only a 30-50 bps headwind to revenue in 2026 (vs. ~100 bps headwind in 2025). With CBI contribution rising and PLS headwinds moderating, the company's structural growth profile should improve meaningfully in the coming years. This, coupled with cyclical recovery, positions it well for growth acceleration.

The improving revenue growth outlook should also help the company's margins, which should benefit from operating leverage. Historically, the company's incremental margins were typically in the 35-40% range. However, management is expecting incremental margins in the mid-to-high 40s for 2026. The years of portfolio optimization through product line simplification and continued execution on enterprise initiatives have resulted in a reduced cost structure for the company, which should help incremental margins moving forward.

Further, the new product developments under the customer-backed innovation initiative should help the company charge better prices, given they solve specific customer problems and face less competition. This should also help the company's margins.

In addition, continued execution on enterprise initiatives, including 80-20 front-to-back optimization, strategic sourcing, and productivity programs, should continue to help. So, I am optimistic about the company's margin growth prospects.

Valuation and Rating

ITW is trading at 26.16x the FY26 consensus EPS estimate of $11.25 and 24.29x the FY27 consensus EPS estimate of $12.12. The company's valuation on FY26 estimates is a slight premium versus its 5-year average forward PE of 24.81x.

I believe this premium is justified given the company's structurally improved growth rate, with CBI contributions continuing to increase while PLS drag decreases. The cyclical recovery further supports a higher valuation multiple given the growth runway ahead.

The company is expected to post high single-digit EPS growth over the coming years, which, coupled with approximately 2.19% dividend yield, should result in high single-digit to low double-digit returns, if we assume the company continues to maintain its current valuation multiple. There is also a potential for upward revision in earnings estimates as cyclical recovery continues to take hold. Overall, I find the risk-reward attractive here, with good execution and cyclical recovery helping the company's growth moving forward. Hence, I will continue to have a buy rating on this stock.

Risks

In addition to organic growth, the company also has a history of doing bolt-on M&A. Inorganic growth is relatively riskier than organic growth, and there are risks related to over paying for an acquisition, integration issues, or not realizing intended synergies. If any of the future acquisitions by the company go wrong, it may negatively impact the stock price.

Another risk is related to the interest rate cycle. I expect interest rate cuts to continue moving forward. However, if interest rate cuts are delayed or are of smaller magnitude than expected, the cyclical recovery I am betting on may be impacted.

Takeaway

ITW is poised to see cyclical recovery moving forward. In addition, the company's structural growth has improved with CBI initiatives gaining traction and PLS drag reducing. Further, its margin should benefit from operating leverage and enterprise initiatives. This should result in good top and bottom line growth moving forward. Hence, I have a buy rating on the stock.

data.title: Sana Biotechnology: A Small, Speculative Play
data.description:
Shares of engineered cell concern Sana Biotechnology, Inc. (SANA) have nearly tripled off their recent lows in early April, mostly on the strength of data from one type 1 diabetes patient who received a precursor to its SC451. The clinical data not only suggest the possibility of a functional cure but also de-risk/validate its approach to escaping immune destruction, which could have applications across many indications.

I last did a deep dive on this name in late 2024. With it abandoning two early-stage programs to pursue a potentially enormous type 1 diabetes indication, yet obstacles still to hurdle just to enter the clinic, Sana Biotechnology merited another look. An updated analysis follows below.

When I investigated Sana in March of 2024, it was advancing two hypoimmune (HIP)-modified CAR T clinical programs against multiple indications, although there appeared to be more hype regarding a preclinical program transplanting allogeneic HIP-modified pancreatic islet cells as a potential cure for type 1 diabetes. Its stock was trading around $7 per share, and investment was not recommended, owing to a $1.3 billion valuation net of cash and data on a total of four patients.

Sana Biotechnology, Inc., is a Seattle-based preclinical-stage biopharmaceutical concern focused on the development of engineered cells to treat disease. It is now preparing to file an IND for type 1 diabetes program SC451 after deprioritizing and eventually dropping two clinical programs over the past 14 months. Sana commenced operations in 2018 and went public in 2021, raising eye-popping net proceeds of $626.4 million at $25 per share. Its stock currently trades just under four bucks a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of $1 billion.

Engineered Cells

For approximately 75 years, allogeneic (donor) stem cell transplantation has been employed to treat a broad spectrum of maladies. There are three sources of allogeneic cells: embryonic stem cells (ESCs), induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs), and donor-derived cells. However, rejection of the transplant by the body’s immune system remains a significant challenge to successful outcomes. To address this issue, a donor is sought that has a similar human leukocyte antigen (HLA) type as its respective recipient, which usually is derived from cells, tissue, or organs of a close relative. However, this method significantly limits availability. Autologous (from the patient) transplantation—encompassing ~58% of the total—also overcomes the autoimmune issues, but it is limited to certain cell types and has truncated scalability. The current standard of care approach to preventing rejection is a lifetime of immunosuppressive medication. Gene modification has been added to the arsenal but with incomplete results to date.

With a goal of creating a universal cell capable of evading immune detection irrespective of cell type or transplant location, Sana believes it has created an effective workaround to the shortcomings of other rejection therapies through its hypoimmune technology platform, which generates ‘engineered cells.’ They begin as allogeneic cells that are then modified to eliminate major histocompatibility (MHC) expression, which is essentially HLA in humans. There are two types of MHC molecules: class I, which is expressed on the surface of all nucleated cells, and class II, which is expressed on antigen presenting cells. By knocking out MHC class I and II expression, T cells from the adaptive immune system do not recognize the peptides on a cell’s surface as foreign and thus don’t target them for elimination in a dynamic known as tolerance.

However, the human body has built-in redundancy through its innate immune system, whereby natural killer cells or macrophages eliminate cells due to lack of MHC expression, known as a ‘missing-self’ response. To address this dynamic and differentiate itself from other gene modification approaches, Sana then genetically modifies the cell to overexpress CD47, a protein that enables cells to evade elimination by the innate immune system.

The company believes that through these three genetic modifications (MHC class I and class II suppression and CD47 overexpression) to transplanted cells, it will provide protection from autoimmune maladies, eliminating the need for lifelong immuno-suppressive therapy.

Change in Pipeline Direction

Initially, Sana was advancing a pair of HIP-modified allogeneic CAR T-cell therapies, SC291 against systemic lupus erythematosus and anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody-associated vasculitis, as well as SC262 against non-Hodgkin lymphoma. However, owing to what management believes is the curative capacity of its type 1 diabetes candidate, as well as significant potential for its in-vivo fusogen platform, it deprioritized and eventually halted further development of SC291 and SC262 over the past 14 months.

The company’s pivot was actuated by clinical results of UP421, a precursor to its current preclinical asset SC451. It is an HIP-modified stem-cell-derived pancreatic islet cell therapy for the treatment of type 1 diabetes (T1D), a malady where the immune system attacks beta cells within the pancreatic islet of Langerhans, rendering the insulin pumps ineffective. For those unaware, insulin is a hormone that permits cells to metabolize glucose to produce energy. Without it, no cell in the body can use or store glucose normally. The malady afflicts 9.4 million globally, the lives of whom are characterized by heart and blood vessel issues; kidney, nerve, and eye damage; foot amputations due to poor blood flow; and a truncated lifespan by ~10 to ~15 years predominantly from heart attacks and strokes.

Typically presenting between four and 14 years of age, T1D has no cure, with most sufferers dying quickly from cellular starvation before the discovery (1921) and manufacture (1923) of insulin. Since that time, the condition has been treated via two-to-four daily insulin injections or by pumps that deliver round-the-clock therapy through a catheter under the skin. Pancreatic islet transplants from recently deceased donors have demonstrated long-term glucose control; however, their supply is relatively limited, and the procedure requires a lifetime of immunosuppression, which is essentially replacing one lifelong need with another. Stem cell-derived islets—most notably from Vertex’s (VRTX) clinical program, zimislecel—have potential for scale but suffer the same need to stunt both allogenic and autoimmune rejection. Sana believes it can provide long-term glucose control at scale while eliminating the need for immunosuppression with its HIP modifications—essentially a functional cure. So sure of the therapeutic value of its preclinical therapy and its market potential—and also in a nod to diminishing cash—the company suspended its other clinical programs.

That confidence initially came from preclinical research encompassing one non-human primate, whose circulating serum C-peptide (a biomarker indicating insulin production) had normalized one week after transplantation, remaining that way for six months. More importantly, blood glucose levels were tightly controlled over that period, during which the primate was completely insulin-independent. Furthermore, there was no immune response. These results have more or less been repeated in murine models at more than 15 months.

Then, in an investigator-sponsored, open-label, one-patient Phase 1 study out of Sweden, a single dose of HIP-engineered insulin-producing pancreatic cells using Sana’s technology—dubbed UP421—demonstrated survival and function, as measured by circulating C-peptide at six months. Furthermore, C-peptide increased during a mixed meal tolerance test at day 28, consistent with normal insulin secretion. Furthermore, an immune analysis using donor islet cells and patient immune cells revealed that the fully edited HIP islet cells had evaded both T cell (adaptive) and natural killer cell (innate) responses, with no donor-specific antibodies detected at day 28. Keep in mind that these results come from a dosage estimated at 7% of what is normal, as the main objective of the study was to demonstrate immune evasion and some function. It should be noted that the drug product used in the one-patient Swedish study contained a mixture of partially and fully edited islet cells. There was evidence of immune response to (rejection of) the partially edited cells.

Initial four-week data announced in January 2025 sent shares of SANA soaring 342% (intraday) to $7.30. They round-tripped to $1.26 by April but have since caught a bid, more than tripling, largely on the back of confirmatory six-month UP421 data in July.

Although these one-patient data points are potentially transformative for T1D patients with applications across many disease types, Sana is still solving the manufacturing-at-scale issue. Attempting to gene modify a PSC and expand it into myriad doses requires genomic stability (no mutations) and product purity (only pancreatic islet cells). As such, the company is developing an HIP-edited master cell bank, the output of which should be supportive of a 12-to-15-patient Phase 1 trial commencing sometime in 2026. It should also be noted that Vertex is developing a device to shield implanted insulin-producing cells (like its zimislecel) from the body’s immune system and HIP-modified stem cells (like Sana’s), both in the preclinic.

Sana’s other pivot involves an emphasis on its in-vivo fusogen platform, in which it engineers a virus-like particle to deliver a single CAR T payload (known as a fusosome) into a T cell to attack a host of different B-cell mediated blood cancers and autoimmune disorders without the need for lymphodepleting chemotherapy, which is a feature of ex-vivo manufactured CAR T cells. The likely first clinical manifestation from its fusogen platform is SG293, a fusosome that delivers genetic material to CD8+ T cells so it can create CD19-directed CAR T cells while avoiding off-target toxicities. With complete phenotypic reset of B cells in non-human primates without lymphodepletion providing great promise in the preclinical setting, Sana hopes to commence human trials with SG293 in 2027.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

To pay for the advancement of these potentially game-changing technologies into the clinic, Sana executed a secondary offering in August 2025, raising net proceeds of $80.6 million at $3.35 per share cash. With cash and securities totaling $153.1 million as of September 30, 2025, and a cash burn of $40 million a quarter prior to the suspension of its SC291 and SC262 programs, it has a somewhat limited operating runway to YE26.

Street analysts are almost unanimously receptive to SC451’s potential, as can be seen above.

Verdict:

Sana’s approach holds enormous potential, and owing to this dynamic, the market has already assigned a $1 billion valuation to its essentially preclinical assets. That said, if it provides a functional cure for T1D and validates its technology in upcoming clinical trials, the company will be worth many multiples of its current share price. That said, it will have to continue to dilute shareholders over the foreseeable future, possibly at a much higher share price. As such, Sana should be considered a LEAP (greater than a one-year option contract), with substantial upside that may dampen over time through dilution or zero out if its technology proves feckless. The risk-reward asymmetry suggests a small investment, but only for risk-tolerant investors now, with an eye on adding either through cost-averaging or data derisking in the future. I have taken a small position in SANA via covered call orders.

data.title: Rates Spark: Dutch Pension Funds May Prepare Early For 2027 Transitions
data.description:
By Benjamin Schroeder, Senior Rates Strategist | Michiel Tukker, Senior European Rates Strategist

Dutch pension fund transition should keep upward pressure on longer rates in 2026

Almost €1tr of Dutch pension assets are scheduled to transition in 2027, but we should start seeing the impact on markets this year. The Dutch news site PensioenPro reported that many smaller funds had already rebalanced their interest rate hedges in December 2025 (or earlier) instead of waiting until their transition moment in January 2026. On the other hand, for PMT – with around €80bn of assets, the second-largest fund that transitioned – the transition period is planned for the first six months in 2026 and therefore should still bring some flows. PFZW, the largest fund, which transitioned (€250bn assets), is more secretive about its transition strategy.

The flows from pension funds at the start of this year likely disappointed those with 10s30s steepener positions, which may help to explain the significant flattening in mid-January. Interviews with pension funds suggest that many did push for a quick rebalancing of hedges (one fund even finished by 6 January), but the amount of unwinding was not as much as initially expected. Some funds actually needed to increase hedges, allowing bilateral exchanges of positions without having to access public markets. One reason cited for the higher hedging needs was the strong rise in rates at the end of 2025. As we wrote earlier, estimating the exact flows is difficult and can change depending on market moves.

Since mid-January, the EUR 10s30s curve has actually steepened more than the US, and we still see more steepening ahead. The Dutch pension reforms should continue to put upward pressure on the long end of the curve as we approach 1 January 2027. An important pension fund to watch will be ABP. With its €530bn assets, it is by far the largest fund to transition.

Friday's events and market view

The main release for the day is the US CPI for January, where the consensus is looking for a 0.3% month-on-month price increase in both the core and headline measures. The decline in the annual rate to 2.5% will also mean that the bullish tone in rates markets is unlikely to be disrupted. The Federal Reserve's Lorie Logan and Stephen Miran will speak, meaning both ends of the hawk-dove spectrum are represented.

The eurozone will release its second estimate for 2025's fourth quarter GDP. The focus will likely be more on the geopolitical backdrop, with the Munich Security Conference kicking off and continuing over the weekend, including an appearance by European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde. Last year, the event served as the beginning of a strategic rethink in the EU that led to ramped-up defence budgets and gave us the EU’s SAFE.

The EU leaders gathering yesterday showed that politicians at least recognise the urgency that action is needed to restore Europe’s competitiveness and growth potential. Views still diverge on the how, as underscored by the opposing views on Eurobonds between France and Germany.

Last night’s 30y Treasury auction marked the last primary market supply for the week. But it saw strong demand amid weakening equity markets.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Private Equity Investment In Fintech Up 44% In 2025
data.description:
Global private equity and venture capital investments in the fintech sector grew 43.7% year-over-year to $18.54 billion in 2025, even as deal volume declined.

The volume of deals fell 34.2% from 2024, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

The convergence of infrastructure and AI is supporting premium valuations for fintech, according to Amjad Ahmad, managing partner at venture capital firm 500 Global.

Some fintech infrastructure can handle the actual flow of funds and are intelligent, adding insight by providing AI analytics for areas such as payments orchestration, cross-border settlement, fraud prevention and identity verification.

"Investors are gravitating toward mission-critical infrastructure platforms that combine financial rails with data intelligence," Ahmad said.

Investment opportunities are shifting to business-to-business fintech companies, which focus on infrastructure, and away from business-to-consumer, where the interface is primary, added Han Ming Ho, lead of the Asia-Pacific Investment Funds practice at law firm Reed Smith LLP in Singapore. Regulatory and compliance tools embedded in fintech infrastructure, for example, tend to attract investors, Ho said.

The trend of AI adding value to fintech solutions is reflected in the median private equity deal size in the sector, which was up about 29% year-over-year to $9 million, according to Market Intelligence data.

Top fintech transactions

Several of 2025's largest private equity-backed fintech deals involved targets that provide infrastructure, such as Binance Holdings Ltd., which offers a platform that enables banks to build and launch cryptocurrency services.

The largest deal in the sector was Clearlake Capital Group LP's $7.49 billion acquisition of data and analytics infrastructure company Dun & Bradstreet Holdings Inc.

The US and Canada were the leading locations for fintech deals in 2025, with 130 transactions totaling about $14.1 billion.

500 Global's Ahmad said North America will continue to be the anchor market for fintech investment, given its "comparatively clearer exit pathways," among others.

"The region continues to set valuation benchmarks, particularly in payments infrastructure, AI-enabled finance automation and digital asset platforms," Ahmad said.

Latin America and the Caribbean ranked second, with a total transaction value of $2.12 billion. The Middle East came in third with $1.25 billion.

The fintech sector in the Middle East, particularly within the Gulf Cooperation Council, is well positioned for more investment, driven by regulatory modernization.

"In the Gulf, coordinated government initiatives, open banking frameworks and strong banking partnerships are accelerating fintech integration into the formal financial system," Ahmad said.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Vornado Realty Dips As Manhattan Office Leasing Moves Up
data.description:
Vornado Realty's (VNO) 30% pullback from its 52-week high through the last few months has come at odds with the continued rise in its occupancy rate as demand for prime office space in Manhattan strengthens. The borough's total vacancy rate at the end of December declined by 300 basis points year-over-year to 13.6%, the lowest total vacancy rate of all U.S. metro areas. The REIT's New York occupancy rate came in at 90% to end 2025, a material 250 basis points sequential improvement from the third quarter, even as 555 California Street saw occupancy dip by over the same period to 88.9% to end the fourth quarter. The upswing in New York leasing volume has placed VNO's occupancy rate back in the 90s, helping management to hit a key objective. I expect this occupancy rate to continue to increase, with most New York business leaders expecting to expand office space within 18 months, according to a recent KPMG survey.

When I last covered VNO in November, the REIT faced uncertainty in New York around political changes, including floated tax increases, and the impact this would have on office space demand. I viewed the transition as immaterial to the broader story of demand for Class A office space, as higher funding rates and office-to-residential conversions push through a fundamental reorientation of the Manhattan office market. Higher funding rates have materially impacted new office construction starts, with 2.34 million square feet under construction as of the end of December. This placed Manhattan, roughly 7.5% of the nearly 31 million square feet of the national pipeline at the end of 2025, but lower than the level of new construction before the pandemic.

Total Returns, FFO, The Preferreds, And Downside Risk

The company last declared a cash dividend of $0.74 per share, an annual payout that was unchanged from the prior year, to drive a 2.45% dividend yield. This is not an income play, with the annual payout, the negative 173 basis points spread to the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) at 4.18%, and a near-term history of cuts, all making it hard to recommend VNO as an income play. The play here is really a recovery of the stock from current lows, in aggregate with the ongoing dividend yield. VNO generated fourth-quarter revenue of $453.71 million, down 0.9% over its year-ago comp but a beat by $13.48 million on consensus estimates. Operating expenses came in at $234 million, down around $7.6 million sequentially from the third quarter and falling from $236 million a year ago.

This drove a net operating income ("NOI") of $243.4 million, up from $235.9 million in the third quarter, but dropping 12% from $276.59 million a year-ago. This was on the back of a lower balance of non-cash adjustments for straight-line rents and amortization of acquired below-market leases a year ago. VNO signed 869,000 square feet of total Manhattan office space during the fourth quarter, with a weighted average lease term of 9.9 years and an initial rent of $95.36. Leases on the 441,000 square feet of second generation relet space expanded 8.1% on a GAAP basis, and 7.2% on a cash basis during the fourth quarter.

Funds from operations ("FFO") came in at $112.92 million, around $0.56 per share, down from $0.58 in the year-ago period. Hence, bears will be right to state that while VNO has seen its occupancy rates tick up, FFO growth continues to lag its prior period on the back of higher interest rates and general and administrative expenses that ticked up 9.4% year-over-year to $40.7 million. This is set against a very well-laddered wall of lease expirations, with the VNO only set to see roughly 6% of its leases in 2026 and another 6.3% in 2027 expire, with a further 21% of its leases through to the end of 2030 expiring after this. The REIT does face heightened debt maturities through the next three years, with 21% of its debt, at around $1.24 billion, coming due for repayment in 2026.

I own a position in the Series O Preferreds (VNO.PR.O). While these are up since I first recommended them as a buy, they've been broadly flat over the last year even in the face of rate cuts, due to long-term U.S. Treasury rates staying stubbornly high. They pay out a $1.1125 per share annual coupon; hence, at $15.10 per share, they offer a current yield of 7.4%. I view these as a low-risk security to lock in 418 basis points of income above the U.S. 10-year for a REIT rated just one notch below investment grade by Fitch Ratings.

Conclusion

VNO's preferreds continue to present a healthy discount of around $10 per share to their redemption price as long-term rates stay high, while paying out a 7.4% current yield. This is a core position as the REIT continues to see its occupancy rate tick up. I am rating VNO as a buy on the back of the dip and the continued strength of its core market.

data.title: Spot The Difference
data.description:
By Joseph Nelesen

From a U.S. equities perspective, the first month of 2026 started on a different trajectory compared to years past. S&P 500® performance trailed much of the rest of the world (as measured by the S&P World Ex-U.S. Index), and within the U.S., the performance of the S&P 500 Information Technology (“U.S. Tech”) ranked in the bottom half among all 11 GICS® sectors. Fears of trade tensions tied to geopolitics, rising worries of an AI bubble and tepid economic data points dragged on overall market performance, with The 500® falling for much of the month before finishing up 1.5%, while U.S. Tech fell 1.7%.

It was just over three months prior that U.S. Tech had reached an all-time high in October 2025, before sliding down 6.1% over the three months ending in January. U.S. Tech’s latest decline is noteworthy but certainly not unprecedented, as the sector produced negative three-month performance 109 times over the last 30 years, with more than half of those periods even worse than January’s result, as shown in Exhibit 1.

One might fairly ask what happened to U.S. Tech after previous three-month declines and how its performance compared to the same sector around the rest of the world at those times. As it turns out, Information Technology showed resilience worldwide, but particularly so in the U.S. Exhibit 2 plots performance of two sector indices, the S&P 500 Information Technology and the S&P World Ex-U.S. Information Technology (Sector) Index (“Ex-U.S. Tech”) over 12-month periods after 3-month declines since January 2006. Following periods of three-month declines, U.S. Tech typically rebounded at a greater magnitude than did Ex-U.S. Tech, increasing an average of 22.1% over the subsequent 12 months and outperforming The 500 by 7.6%, while 3-month drops in Ex-U.S. Tech were followed by average total performance of 12.0% and 1.5% excess performance versus the S&P World Ex-U.S. Index.

Shifting focus away from periods of decline and instead evaluating all periods in the last 20 years, we find that U.S. Tech often tended to lead its global counterparts. During 12-month periods when U.S. Tech was in an outperformance cycle relative to The 500, it simultaneously outperformed the rest of the world (as measured by the S&P World Ex-U.S. Index) 91% of the time, as shown in Exhibit 3. In such periods, U.S. Tech also generated higher performance than Ex-U.S. Tech 88% of the time.

While sector membership explains a significant portion of constituent performance across different parts of the market and the economic cycle, variations in same-sector performance across geographies reveal the importance of understanding more granular constituent categorizations. In Exhibit 4, we begin to uncover potential drivers of U.S. Tech performance relative to its Ex-U.S. Tech counterpart, illustrating significant differences in GICS sub-industry weights. Specifically, the Semiconductors sub-industry comprised over 40% of U.S. Tech’s weight, yet only 8% of Ex-U.S. Tech’s, which held its largest weight (42%) in Semiconductor Materials & Equipment. Among the two sub-industries, Semiconductors significantly outperformed, aided by greater margins and pricing power for firms further down the AI chip value chain.

History has shown that periods of U.S. Tech underperformance have often been followed by periods of relative strength, and drivers of such sector resilience can be better understood through considering additional dimensions of geography and sub-industry composition. Like other sectors, Information Technology continues to evolve and reveal new clues that help to understand and navigate global markets.

Note: The S&P World Index comprises S&P World Ex-U.S. Index and S&P United States LargeMidCap.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
